coredata.dc:title: Transport of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in saturated porous media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Congrong
Guo, Xuan
Gao, Xiangtao
Yu, Zhongbo
Jiang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of carbon-based nanoparticles are widely used in scientific applications in the recent years. With the size less than 10 nm, and the strong hydrophilic characteristics GQDs exhibit high mobility in the environment, and can easily transmit to the groundwater environment. Hence, a good understanding of the transport behaviors of GQDs in porous media can provide guidance for assessment and prediction of its environmental risks. Saturated packed column experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of ionic strength (IS,1 and 100 mM), pH (4,7 and 9) and initial concentration (9 and 18 mg L−1) on the fate and transport of GQDs in saturated porous media. Advection-dispersion-reaction model with the second-order kinetics simulated the process well. The retention of GQDs was promoted with increased ionic strength in sand column, which could be explained with classic filtration and XDLVO theories. The increase of pH slightly reduced the GQDs deposition in saturated porous media, due to that there were no secondary minima in XDLVO calculation, and GQDs still showed high mobility. Higher input concentration slightly increased the GQDs retention due to the ripening process. The recovery rate of GQDs were more than 90 % in the environment with low salt content, exhibiting extremely high migration capacity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fate and transport
Graphene quantum dot
XDLVO theory
Porous media

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM imagines of GQDs(a,b).Stability of GQDs under different experimental conditions(c).XDLVO energy between GQDs particles under various experimental conditions(d).

Observed and best-fit simulated GQDs transport process under various condition: a) IS = 1and 100 mM; c) pH = 4, 7 and 9; e) initial concentration = 9 and 18 mg L−1. XDLVO energy between GQDs and sand corresponding to b) IS and d) pH conditions.
Summary of best-fit model results and recovery rates of GQDs transport in saturated porous media under different experimental conditions.
Zeta-potential of GQDs and sand in background solutions.

head.ce:title: Transport of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in saturated porous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Hydrology Water Resources & Hydraulic Engineering, Hohai University, College of Hydrology & Water Resource, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210098, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Soil and Sustainable Agriculture, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, P.O. Box 821, Nanjing, 210008, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Soil and Sustainable Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Soil and Sustainable Agriculture, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, P. O. Box 821, Nanjing 210008, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of carbon-based nanoparticles are widely used in scientific applications in the recent years. With the size less than 10 nm, and the strong hydrophilic characteristics GQDs exhibit high mobility in the environment, and can easily transmit to the groundwater environment. Hence, a good understanding of the transport behaviors of GQDs in porous media can provide guidance for assessment and prediction of its environmental risks. Saturated packed column experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of ionic strength (IS,1 and 100 mM), pH (4,7 and 9) and initial concentration (9 and 18 mg L−1) on the fate and transport of GQDs in saturated porous media. Advection-dispersion-reaction model with the second-order kinetics simulated the process well. The retention of GQDs was promoted with increased ionic strength in sand column, which could be explained with classic filtration and XDLVO theories. The increase of pH slightly reduced the GQDs deposition in saturated porous media, due to that there were no secondary minima in XDLVO calculation, and GQDs still showed high mobility. Higher input concentration slightly increased the GQDs retention due to the ripening process. The recovery rate of GQDs were more than 90 % in the environment with low salt content, exhibiting extremely high migration capacity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have developed rapidly in scientific applications in the recent years due to their excellent conductivity, magnetism, optical properties, etc. [1]. Graphene is a type of hexagonal planar two-dimensional carbon material with honeycomb lattice composed of carbon atoms and sp2 hybrid orbits, and it exhibits excellent high mechanical strength and good thermal properties [2]. When the transverse size of graphene is reduced to 10 nm, the movement of electrons in the material is limited, which has obvious quantum confinement effect [3]. This nano-sized graphene material is known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs are generally about 10 nm in diameter and 0.5–1.0 nm in thickness. The surface of GQDs contains carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which makes it water-soluble. Since GQDs and its hybrids have high sensitivity and good selectivity, so they play an important role in bioimaging, fluorescence sensing, photocatalysis, fuel cells, electronic devices, agricultural food and other fields [4–13]. The increased use of GQDs, as they are released into the environment, will pose significant threat to the ecosystem, public health and future sustainability of water resources [14].

Underground aquifer is one of the precious resources on the earth. As a new carbon nanomaterial, still very little is understood about GQDs’ deposition and transport processes in porous media. However, numerous investigations have been conducted on the transport behavior of other carbon-based nanomaterials in porous media. The research found factors, such as ionic strength(IS) [15,16], pH [17], initial concentration [18], grain size [19], surface heterogeneities [20], flow rate [21], etc. affect the migration of carbon-based nanoparticles. Based on XDLVO theory, the chemical solution condition---IS and pH, has significant effect on the transport of carbon-based nanomaterials. For fullerenes [22], graphene oxide (GO) [23] and carbon dots (CDs) [24], their deposition process in saturated porous media enhanced with increasing IS. Sharma et al. found at the critical deposition IS of 4 mM NaCl, exceeded 99 % carbon nanotubes (CNTs) was filtrated from a 15 cm length column [25].Wang et al. found that sensitivity of GO to temperature was depending on IS. At low IS, temperature has almost no effect on the retention and transport of GO, but when the IS increases to 10 mM, the temperature showed significant effect on the retention and migration of GO in saturated porous media [26]. In term of pH, both Tian et al. and Sharma et al. proved that the increase of pH value resulted in the increase of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) mobility [25,27]；however, graphene (GR) is not sensitive to the pH condition. Liu et al. found that graphene (GR) has a high mobility in acid-cleaned sand, regardless of whether the pH is acidic or alkaline, although the recovery rate under alkaline condition is 1.1 % higher than under acidic condition [17].

There are no consistent conclusions on the effect of input concentration on the mobility of carbon-based nanomaterials. Sun et al. showed that higher input concentration of GO produced 'blocking' mechanism, and as a result reduced the particle retention rate [28]. On the contrary, Zhang et al. found when IS greater than 0.1 mM, higher GO input concentrations resulted in increased colloid retention [29].

There has been no studies investigated the fate and transport of GQDs, only carbon dots were illustrated by Kamrani et al. that the mobility of carbon dots(CDs) in saturated porous media, increased with the increase of pH, the decrease of IS, the increase of sand particle size and the increase of initial concentration [24]. Both GQDs and CDs are nanoparticles with size smaller than 10 nm, but the structure of GQD is similar to layered structure, while CDs is spherical structure. In contrast to the hydrophobicity of CDs, GQDs have good hydrophilicity and high mobility [30,31]. Thus, their transport behaviors would be different. Since IS, pH and initial concentration are the key factors to understand the retention and transport of nanoparticles in porous media, the objectives of this study are, 1)understanding the effect of IS on GQDs transport in saturated porous media;2)exploring the retention of GQDs in saturated porous media under neutral, acidic and alkaline conditions of pH; 3)investigating the effects of initial input concentration on GQDs migration in saturated porous media, and at the end environmental guidance is provided.

GQDs used were purchased from manufacturer (ALADDIN Biochemical Technology, Inc.), with layered diameter range of 3−6 nm. Its density is 1 mg/cm3 and stock concentration 1000 mg/L. GQDs size were measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). The GQDs input concentration levels of 18 mg/L or 9 mg/L in the experiment, were adjusted by diluting the stock GQDs suspension. Before injection to the column, GQDs were sonicated (KH-500DB) for 2 h for thorough dispersion. Sodium chloride (NaCl, premium purity, China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.) was added to adjust the solution to the designed ionic strength of 1 mM or 100 mM. The pH values of the background solutions were 4, 7 and 9, adjusted by hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytical purity, China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH, analytical purity, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.). The stability of GQDs suspension was determined by monitoring the absorbance of GQDs suspension by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (MAPADA, UV-1200 spectrophotometer) at wavelength of 250 nm every half hour for two hours. The results showed that the GQDs suspensions were stable. Zeta potential and hydro-dynamic diameter of GQDs suspension under each working condition were measured by Zeta Sizer (Malvern Nano Z) and Nanotrac wave II (Microtrac Inc., Nanotrac wave II), respectively.

Potassium nitrate (KNO3, analytical purity, China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.) was used as a conservative tracer and measured by ultraviolet spectrophotometer at 220 nm wavelength. The nitrate was added to the GQDs suspension to achieve a concentration of 30 mg/L.

Quartz sand (Standard Sand & Silica Co.) screened to size range of 0.6 to 0.8 mm was used as experimental porous media in this study. The quartz sand was washed repeatedly with tap water to remove surface dust. Then it was washed and immersed in 10 % nitric acid (v:v) for 24 h, and turned over every 12 h, to remove the metal oxides and other impurities. Then quartz sand was rinsed with demonized water for 2–3 times again, and then dried at 105 °C before use. The Zeta potential of sand was measured referring to the method of Johnson et al. [32]. Firstly, 5 g quartz sand was put into a small beaker and 20 mL background solution was added to it. After 10 min of ultrasonic treatment, the surface potential of the supernatant of the beaker determined by Zeta Sizer represent the zeta potential of the sand.

Column experiments were conducted in a 15.0 cm high sand column with 3.0 cm inner diameter. A stainless-steel mesh with pore size of 50 μm was installed at the inlet and outlet of the column, to prevent the escape of the sand and still allow the flow of water without blocking the pipes. The sand porosity was 0.38. GQDs suspensions were pumped from the bottom to the top of the column by a peristaltic pump (HL-2B) at a flow rate of 1.5 mL/min.

The saturated sand column was first flushed with DI water, and then at least 20 PVs of background solutions with desired ionic strength and pH condition, until the absorbance of the effluent at the 250 nm was the same as background solution. After that, the GQDs suspensions were injected for 3 PVs, followed by about 3 PVs background solution until the GQDs absorbance of the effluents was 0. An automatic fraction collector (EBS-20) was set to collect the effluent samples every 3 min. The concentration of GQDs in the effluent was measured immediately. Column experiments under all working conditions were repeated twice.

The transport of GQDs in saturated porous media could be described as:
(1)

(3)

Where c is the nanoparticles concentration in the aqueous phase [ML−3], s is the solid phase nanoparticles concentration [MM-1], D is the hydrodynamic dispersion coefficient [L2T−1],
k a
is the first-order attachment coefficient [T-1],
k d
is the first-order detachment coefficient [T-1], ψ is a dimensionless nanoparticle retention function,
s
m a x
is the maximum solid phase concentration [MM-1],θ is the volumetric water content[dimensionless], t is the time [T],z is the travel distance in the direction of flow [L],q is the Darcy velocity [LT−1],and ρ is the porous medium bulk density [ML−3].The best fitting values of
s
m a x
,
k a
and
k d
were obtained by inverse fitting of breakthrough curves with the Formulas (1)–(3). The concentration breakthrough curve of GQDs transport experiments were simulated by HYDRUS-1D model.

Extended Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (XDLVO)theory was used to describe interactions forces between GQDs-GQDs and between GQDs and quartz sand particles. The interaction forces include: van der Waals (VDW) attraction, electrostatic repulsion (EL) and Lewis acid-base (AB) interaction [33]. The total XDLVO energy of interaction can be written as:
(4)

X D L V O
=
Φ

Φ

Φ

(5)
Φ
V D W
= −
A
12 π
h 2
(6)
Φ
E L
= 32
ε 0
ε r
κ
γ 1
γ 2
k T
ν e
2
e x p
− κ h
(7)
Φ
A B
= Δ
G
h 0
π
α 2
e x p
h 0
− h
λ
where h is the separation distance between nanoparticles and sand particles, and A is the Hamaker constant,
γ i
= tan h ( ν e
ψ i
/ k T )
, ψ is the surface potential, k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, ν is the valence of the electrolyte, e is the electron charge,
ε 0
is the permittivity of vacuum,
ε r
is the relative permittivity of the solution, κ is the Debye-Huckel parameter, λ is the characteristic decay length of AB interactions in solution, α is the radius of nanoparticle,
h 0
is the minimum equilibrium cutoff distance and
Δ
G
h 0
is the free energy components per unit area between nanoparticle and sand particle [33] [34].

The relative peak concentration (Cmax/C0) of GQDs with low ionic strength was a constant plateau (Fig. 2a). The peak Cmax/C0 values of the GQDs BTC with the higher solution ionic strength 100 mM, was less than 0.5 at the beginning, and then increased gradually to almost 0.8, which was quite close to the peak concentration value when the ionic strength was 1 mM (Fig. 2a). The increasing trend of the peak concentration value of higher IS might be explained by the blocking mechanism, which is the second minima deposition points was occupied and less GQDs could be deposited with more GQDs went through the column. Meanwhile, tailing of BTC was higher than when solution ionic strength was higher. It was due to the secondary minima energy was small and could be easily disturbed by the dynamic flow, resulting in more GQDs released.

Increased ionic strength also extended the initial elution time of GQDs from 0.9 PV to 1.01 PV, when ionic strength increased from 1 mM to 100 mM. It might be caused by the decrease of electrostatic repulsion force between GQDs particles and between GQDs and quarts sand, which was benefit to the aggregation of particles into larger particles while passing through the column. As can be seen from Table 2, the hydro-dynamic diameter of GQDs increases from 132.7 nm to 243.5 nm. Larger particles could be easily demobilized by straining and gravitational settling in the sand media, which slowed down transport rate, resulting in a longer initial elution time.

Simulation of the mathematical model described the experimental data of bromide and GQDs at the two ionic strength very well (Fig. 2a), with R2> 0.99 (Table 1). The optimized
k d
and
k a
increased with increased ionic strength (Table 1) from 0.004 to 0.0065 min−1 and from 0.0052 to 0.0279 min−1, respectively, confirming the promoting effect of ionic strength on the GQDs retention. Sma
x increased from 0.07 to 0.11ug/g, indicating the adsorption capacity increased with higher ionic strength.

The high mobility of GQDs did not vary much whether it was acidic, neutral or alkaline condition in the experiment (Fig. 2c). When the pH increased from 4 to 9, the recovery rate slightly increased from 91.07%–95.44 % (Table 1). It was due to that the absolute value of GQDs zeta potential and sand zeta potential increased with increasing pH (Table 2), which resulted in increased repulsion force between GQDs and sand, therefore decreased GQDs deposition on sand. However, GQDs hydro-dynamic diameter were almost invariable, indicating it was difficult to overcome the energy barrier. In addition, there was no second minimum energy trap (Fig. 2d), pH did not impact the mobility of the GQDs much.

The relative peak concentration (Cmax/C0) of the BTCs in all pH conditions exceeded 0.8 (Fig. 2c), showing the high mobility of the GQDs. The initial elution time of GQDs was the same as that of tracer. The rising slope at pH = 4 was not as steep as that when pH was 7 and 9, indicating that more GQDs could be deposited on the sand column at the beginning in the acidic condition. The tailing of all BTCs were the same, showing no release of GQDs at the flushing process.

The model also fitted the experimental data of different pH conditions very well (Fig 2c). The optimized
k d
under acidic conditions showed little difference with those under neutral and alkaline conditions. In addition, the optimized
k a
was five times larger when pH = 4 than those when pH = 7 and 9, illustrating straining might contribute to the deposition efficiency coefficient, which was beneficial for GQDs to be immobilized by quartz sand column. It was confirmed by the increased hydro-dynamic size of GQDs under acid condition, for which more GQDs were trapped in the gap between the sand grains.

GQDs have high mobility under acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions when IS was 1 mM. It was consistent with the experiment result of carboxyl-functionalized GR transporting through saturated porous column [17]. XDLVO theory can predict well on CDs and GQDs transport behaviors [24]. GQDs are not easily controlled by pH under low IS conditions. It may be necessary to increase acidity and IS to inhibit the transport of GQDs.

High concentration and low concentration of GQDs running in the experiment exhibit high mobility at low ionic strength (Fig. 2e). The mobility of GQDs decreased slightly with the increase of the concentration (Fig. 2e). With the increase of injecting concentration, the relative peak concentration (Cmax/C0) of GQDs decreased from 0.96 to 0.90, and the recovery rate decreased from 94.16%–93.84 % (Table 1). It could be explained by straining and ripening mechanism. High concentration may increase the probability of collision between GQDs, and lead to aggregation when GQDs pass through sand column. Pore throats are too small to allow aggregated large particles to pass, which was known as straining mechanism [36]. Besides, the reduction of recovery at high concentration may also be caused by ripening mechanism. The deposited GQDs particles may act as new adsorption sites, thus promoting the deposition of GQDs in the sand column [37].

The model simulation results indicated that
k a
and
k d
were higher in the high concentration experiment than in the low concentration of GQDs experiment. It proved that straining and ripening may lead to more GQDs deposited in porous media when concentration was higher. In practical application, higher concentration of GQDs released into the environment may inhibit the migration of GQDs in soil.

In this study, a number of experiments were conducted to study the transport and removal of GQDs in saturated porous media under different ionic strength, pH and initial concentration conditions. A mathematical model was used to simulate the experimental data and help explore the transport and removal mechanism. It was found that ionic strength is the key factor in determining the migration of GQDs in saturated porous media. The retention rate of GQDs increased significantly with the increase of ionic strength due to the decreased zeta potential of the GQDs and the sand, in addition to the emergence of second energy minima. Under low ionic strength condition, GQDs exhibited extremely high mobility regardless of the change of pH value or initial concentration. With the increase of pH, the negative zeta potential of GQDs and sand increases, leading to the increased repulsive force between particles and sand increases, which inhibited the deposition of GQDs in saturated porous media. Because of ripening mechanisms, GQDs in higher concentration was easily removed by the porous media. In the environment with low salt content, GQDs are not sensitive to the change of experimental conditions, and the recovery rates were more than 90 %, showing extremely high migration ability.

Our findings suggested that GQDs can be retained in high ionic strength environment such as soils with high salinity and fragility. Increase the ionic strength of the solution environment could inhibit the mobility of the GQDs and to reduce its environmental risk.

CRY and XG wrote the manuscript, XG conducted the experiments, JJ provided the Zeta potential data, XTG and ZBY provided important advices for the investigation. All authors reviewed the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51509069); the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China (Grant No. 2019B10814); the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Hydrology-Water Resources and Hydraulic Engineering, Hohai University (Grant No. 2017490811), the Special Fund of State Key Laboratory of Hydrology-Water Resources and Hydraulic Engineering, Hohai University (Grant No. 20195025612).
